question: How do ReAct-style agents differ from planner-executor agents?

heuristic_evaluation.question: How do ReAct-style agents differ from planner-executor agents?

claim_lines: ReAct-style agents interleave reasoning and acting: they emit a short reasoning step, call a tool, read the observation, and then decide the next step. Planner-executor agents separate those phases: they first create an explicit plan or decomposition, then execute the steps, often revising the plan when new information arrives. https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629 https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/agents https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview

Tool-use implication: ReAct is tightly coupled to immediate observations, so tool choice is made step-by-step inside the loop. Planner-executor is more structured, so tool calls are sequenced against a plan, which is better for delegation, checkpoints, and longer workflows. That second sentence is an engineering inference from the docs and should be treated as guidance, not a universal fact. https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/agents https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.05772

Failure modes differ too. ReAct can be prompt-sensitive; a later analysis found that performance was minimally influenced by interleaving itself and more by exemplar-query similarity. Planner-executor can fail if the initial plan is bad or a subtask is infeasible; ADaPT argues that both iterative executors and plan-and-execute systems can break when a subtask cannot be executed. https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.13966 https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.05772

Recommendation: use ReAct when you want a tight tool-feedback loop for a short or uncertain task; use planner-executor when the task is long-horizon, multi-step, or benefits from explicit decomposition and stateful coordination. This is a synthesis from the sources, not a hard benchmark rule. https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/agents https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.05772
- interleaved reasoning and acting — ReAct alternates reasoning and tool calls: https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629 https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/agents
- explicit planning stage — planner-executor/deep-agent style systems start with planning and decomposition: https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.05772

- tool-use implications — ReAct makes tool use reactive; planner-executor makes it plan-sequenced and easier to delegate/control: https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/agents https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview
- failure modes — ReAct prompting is brittle/sensitive, and plan-and-execute can fail when subtasks are not executable: https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.13966 https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.05772

- when each architecture is preferable — ReAct fits tighter feedback-loop tasks; planner-executor fits long-horizon, multi-step work: https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/agents

coverage_audit.reason: Clearly explains ReAct as alternating reasoning, tool calls, and observations.
Clearly contrasts planner-executor as planning/decomposition first, then execution.
Addresses reactive vs plan-sequenced tool use and delegation/control implications, though some of this is framed as inference rather than directly supported fact.
Mentions prompt sensitivity for ReAct and brittle plans/infeasible subtasks for planner-executor, but the comparison is somewhat high-level.
Gives a reasonable task-based recommendation: short/uncertain tasks for ReAct, long-horizon/multi-step tasks for planner-executor.

citation_audit.claim: ReAct-style agents interleave reasoning and acting: they emit a short reasoning step, call a tool, read the observation, and then decide the next step.
citation_audit.reason: This is the core ReAct framing and is plausibly supported by the ReAct paper and agent docs.

citation_audit.claim: Planner-executor agents separate those phases: they first create an explicit plan or decomposition, then execute the steps.
citation_audit.reason: Plan-and-execute style systems are directly relevant to this claim.

citation_audit.claim: ReAct is tightly coupled to immediate observations, so tool choice is made step-by-step inside the loop.
citation_audit.reason: This follows from the interleaved ReAct loop and is a fair inference from the cited sources.

citation_audit.claim: Planner-executor is more structured, so tool calls are sequenced against a plan, which is better for delegation, checkpoints, and longer workflows.
citation_audit.reason: The structured sequencing is plausible, but the benefits listed are partly engineering interpretation rather than directly established by the cited sources.

citation_audit.claim: ReAct can be prompt-sensitive; a later analysis found that performance was minimally influenced by interleaving itself and more by exemplar-query similarity.
citation_audit.reason: This is a specific empirical claim that the cited paper is plausibly intended to support.

citation_audit.claim: Planner-executor can fail if the initial plan is bad or a subtask is infeasible.
citation_audit.reason: This is aligned with known plan-and-execute failure modes and is plausibly supported by the cited work.

citation_audit.claim: Use ReAct when you want a tight tool-feedback loop for a short or uncertain task; use planner-executor when the task is long-horizon, multi-step, or benefits from explicit decomposition and stateful coordination.
citation_audit.reason: Good practical advice, but it is presented as a synthesis rather than a directly evidenced rule, so support is only partial.

source_audit.reason: Relevant technical paper for plan-and-execute / agent architecture trade-offs.
Relevant empirical follow-up on ReAct limitations.
Official framework documentation, relevant to planner-executor style systems.
Same source but with a trailing punctuation artifact; likely a citation formatting issue.
Official agent framework documentation, relevant to the comparison.

judge_evaluation.summary: A solid, well-structured comparison that covers all required aspects with mostly relevant sources. The main weakness is that some tool-use and preference claims are presented as synthesis/inference rather than directly evidenced facts.
judge_evaluation.recommended_fix: Tighten the distinction between directly supported claims and inferred guidance, especially for the tool-use advantages and 'when to use which' recommendation.
